content:
I'm curious about people's thoughts. If the L1 token is  stronger than the majority of application tokens built on the L1, isn't this long term destructive? As the L1 becomes more expensive this erodes even more value away from all applications.

Would it not be better for a L1 token to perform below the average application token or only grow in price with the growth of the number of successful applications?
Should the L1 not be like holding cash or gold in your network, more stable so that money is driven naturally towards application tokens for higher returns?
If Z.ink is more successful than ATLAS and POLIS why would a player not sell their ATLAS or POLIS returns for Z.ink, which could in turn drive the earning for playing Star Atlas down with both higher fees (yes still much lower than now) a pour ATLAS/Z.ink token pair.

author: ZiggyMUD
content: IMO: An L1 token should be a resource like oil or gas. The token allows access to a service/feature - ability to modify the state of the global computer. If the approx value of modifying the global computer exceeds the value of transactions that a particular app needs to do, that app will have a hard time succeeding.  Also, if the L1 token is highly speculative, it's going to cause this equation to change a lot. Which is bad for apps. It would be like electricity or gas changing multiple percentages on the daily, with massive swings. Those types of changes are really tough for the consumer / user.

author: defiantwyvern
content:
One issue is that L1s host a ton of different apps, which require different amounts of transactions. So if you have a highly popular app that only requires a few transactions, and those transactions are valuable, the L1 could speculatively rise because people are willing to pay more real value to get those transactions through. (even though they may not actually need much of the L1 token)

And this would make apps w/ lots of transactions suffer

author: SCORMUD
content: Yeah I think the L1 token should control its price based on its application's health, or like GDP in the real world. The world is not very strong right now so OPEC+ releases more oil to drive down oil prices helping the economy have lower operating costs. I'm not saying this system doesn't have its flaws but people cheering Solana price to go higher are in turn asking for the destruction of Solana applications.  Z.ink will have the same problem. Token inflation should be tied to user growth/chain usage/etc. a basket of metrics.  As an ecosystem a high valuation for Z.ink day one is fine because that will be the benchmark price for operations, but future growth of the tokens price needs to be tied to the growth of Star Atlas (and future apps) growth. If Star Atlas is growing and ATLAS tokenomics are well designed then you should want ATLAS to rise ever so slightly faster than Z.ink to create a healthy Star Atlas or application economy IMO.  POLIS is the only token that could go to the moon or zero without directly impacting the economy other than sentimentality. It's like Nvidia's stock, no matter how high it goes it does not directly affect the pricing of any of their products.  At least this is how I see things and I'm not brilliantly smart like many of you here

author: ZiggyMUD
content:
A question I asked Grok:
If you were a investor with no bias which project token would you buy $ILV, $WILD, ATLAS or $POLIS?

"I'd buy $ILV. It stands out with the strongest fundamentals: a fully operational game generating real revenue (directly benefiting holders via buybacks), superior liquidity (~25x $WILD's volume), and proven price momentum (e.g., 60% surges from events). While $WILD has exciting roadmap catalysts for a potential double, Illuvium's maturity reduces downside risk in a volatile 2025 market. $ATLAS and $POLIS feel too tied to an underperforming ecosystem with supply overhangs. This isn't advice—crypto is high-risk; DYOR and consider diversification."

Can you expand on your views on Atlas and Polis from this analysis?

"Why I’d Still Pass on Both Despite their potential, $ATLAS and $POLIS remain high-risk compared to $ILV or $WILD. Star Atlas’s prolonged development cycle and unproven game (still pre-alpha) raise execution concerns. The massive $ATLAS supply and ZINK’s unclear impact add uncertainty, while $POLIS’s governance-only utility limits its appeal unless the DAO scales significantly. $ILV, by contrast, has a live game and revenue model, and $WILD offers a clearer 2025 roadmap with broader metaverse appeal. For $ATLAS or $POLIS to compete, Star Atlas needs a breakout beta release or major player adoption by mid-2025—possible but speculative."

I don't agree with all the points, the answers were much long and these are just the conclusions. However many investors, speculators, and gamblers now use AI to help them with their investment decisions, so AI's opinions actually do matter now. Just food for thought.

author: ZiggyMUD
content:
Strengths and Weaknesses answers from Grok:
"Strengths
-Governance Power: $POLIS holders control key decisions, such as funding development or securing NFT assets. Recent POLIS lockers (Q3 2025) allow users to lock tokens for enhanced security, increasing utility.
-Recent Price Momentum: Up ~6% in the last 24 hours, per CoinGecko, likely tied to Solana gaming buzz and Star Atlas airdrop campaigns.
-Lower Supply Risk: With a max supply of 360M (vs. $ATLAS’s 36B), $POLIS faces less dilution pressure. Its market cap could grow more efficiently with adoption.
-Ecosystem Synergy: $POLIS value is tied to Star Atlas’s success. If the MMO gains traction, governance demand could spike as players and investors seek influence.

Weaknesses
-Derivative Value: $POLIS has no standalone utility outside Star Atlas. If the game underperforms or ZINK shifts focus, $POLIS demand could collapse.
-Low Liquidity: Trading volume (~$0.5M) is even lower than $ATLAS, making it prone to sharp swings. Thin order books increase risk for investors.
-Limited Adoption: The DAO is active but small, with ~10,000 unique holders (per Solana explorer data). Governance tokens often struggle to maintain value without broad participation.
-Development Dependency: Like $ATLAS, $POLIS relies on Star Atlas delivering a functional game. Delays or bugs could erode confidence, as seen in X posts questioning roadmap timelines."

I would be curious what other AI tools answers are?

author: [AEP] Mendizo
content:
It's not a black and white trust AI or not, but every savvy investor is leveraging AI as a second option. We do multiple multi-million dollar investments a year and we use AI in every due diligence report. It can flag things we need to take a deeper look on.

For SA though, as Bryan and others have noted, this is not a project any run of the mill investor would touch, so the question to AI itself is a tad off. 

If someone who knows crypto gaming is ready to invest and is just trying to choose which project, then they would also be digging deeper into each project. The real question then becomes things like "How do I actually make money right now, how easy/automated is it, how liquid is it, what's my investment needed for X return, etc." Right now AI cannot answer any of those questions for any project with accuracy... which is part of the onboarding problem as well since it means potential investors also have a hard time finding the exact info.

author: SCORMUD
content: chatgpt 5 w/ thinking mode  If you were a investor with no bias which project token would you buy $ILV, $WILD, ATLAS or $POLIS? ChatGPT said: Thought for 2m 22s  Short answer: ILV.  Why: among the four, Illuvium (ILV) has (a) a fixed-cap supply (~10M max), (b) live games with ongoing events, and (c) a revenue-share mechanic where protocol revenue is periodically distributed to ILV stakers—giving clearer value capture than a pure in-game currency. On risk-adjusted terms, that’s the cleanest setup here.  GAM3S.GG +3 machinations.io +3 Staking +3  If you forced me to choose a Star Atlas token, I’d pick POLIS over ATLAS:  POLIS = fixed supply (360M), governance/voting via locking (“ve” model), and a live DAO portal/lockers; supply overhang is smaller than ATLAS and the utility is governance/treasury control.  CoinGecko +3 Medium +3 support.staratlas.com +3  ATLAS = the inflationary in-game currency; success hinges on growth + sinks, and it’s historically drawn down hard—fine for playing the game/economy, less compelling as an investment asset.  support.staratlas.com +2 Blockworks +2  Where WILD sits: Wilder World has Super Early Access live on the Epic Games Store and the token is standard ERC-20 with a 500M max supply. It’s a higher-beta bet on execution/momentum; token value capture is still being proven.  Esports Insider +2 Epic Games Store +2  Quick numbers today (rounded):  ILV: ~$145M mkt cap, ~9.5M circulating.  CoinGecko  WILD: ~$100M mkt cap, ~425M circulating, 500M max.  CoinMarketCap  ATLAS: ~$17M mkt cap, ~21.4B circulating, 36B total (inflationary).  CoinMarketCap +1  POLIS: ~$21M mkt cap, ~310M circulating, 360M max. If you want, I can size a simple barbell (e.g., ILV + POLIS core, optional WILD flier) to match your risk tolerance.``

author: SCORMUD
content: Big differences are jurisdiction and risk tolerance. Illuvium is domiciled in Australia, ATMTA is registered in the US. Our legal counsel has always advised a conservative approach to tokenomics, particularly those that could lead to the token being categorized as a security. Revenue distribution is one of those mechanics that lean towards “security.” So, we’ve avoided it. That said, the regulatory landscape is changing rapidly, particularly in the US. Our dream has always been to have some value distribution mechanism for POLIS holders.

author: ZiggyMUD
content: its a bit different   Dual-Currency System • LUX – in-game money (off-chain, used for gameplay) • EVE Token ($EVE) – on-chain token, tradable & used for broader ecosystem   Smart Assemblies (Player-Built Objects) • Players can create bases, turrets, storage, quest-givers, etc. • Custom rules & logic (e.g. “attack enemies”, “trade only with allies”) • Built on CCP’s Carbon Development Platform → planned to be partly open-source   Player-Driven Economies • Guilds / factions can issue their own currencies or resources • Define accepted payment types inside their zones • Enables localized economies tied to the main $EVE token   Seamless Web3 Integration • Wallets run in the background (no complex crypto setup) • Players interact with blockchain features naturally (e.g. crafting, trading)

reply.preview: its a bit different Dual-Currency System • LUX – in-game money (off-chain, used for gameplay) • EVE Token ($EVE) – on-chain token, tradable & used for broader ecosystem Smart Assemblies (Player-Built Objects) • Players can create bases, turrets, storage, quest-givers, etc. • Custom rules & logic (e.g. “attack enemies”, “trade only with allies”) • Built on CCP’s Carbon Development Platform → planned to be partly open-source Player-Driven Economies • Guilds / factions can issue their own currencies or resources • Define accepted payment types inside their zones • Enables localized economies tied to the main $EVE token Seamless Web3 Integration • Wallets run in the background (no complex crypto setup) • Players interact with blockchain features naturally (e.g. crafting, trading)

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: I'm really curious to see the team's plans to address these items. I know we had a TH and a couple brews talking about C4, but I'm not sure I've heard specific conversations surrounding the types of goals that you're mentioning here.  For example - making it difficult for large players to execute every game loop - I don't think expanding the crafting trees will accomplish this goal, at least not on its own. Players can scale infinitely, and there's no real limiting factor. I think Chris has touched on this a bit, but again I don't recall any specific ideas or solutions that the team is considering.  All that to say - I think you've mentioned several incredibly important topics. I'd love to hear some deep dive discussion from the team on how they plan to tackle these things. If this discussion happens early enough, perhaps the community can provide feedback and help avoid some pitfalls that the team hasn't considered.

For example - making it difficult for large players to execute every game loop - I don't think expanding the crafting trees will accomplish this goal, at least not on its own. Players can scale infinitely, and there's no real limiting factor. I think Chris has touched on this a bit, but again I don't recall any specific ideas or solutions that the team is considering. All that to say - I think you've mentioned several incredibly important topics. I'd love to hear some deep dive discussion from the team on how they plan to tackle these things. If this discussion happens early enough, perhaps the community can provide feedback and help avoid some pitfalls that the team hasn't considered.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: Thanks for the response! Playing devil's advocate and working with incomplete knowledge - it sounds like those points are primarily focused on complexity through scaling - making the production chain so big that it's hard to scale your operations to do everything.  I'm curious to hear if anyone has mapped this out from a theoretical perspective. Given that some meta will exist, what is the required scale to produce that meta without sourcing?  I fully acknowledge that this is not an apples-to-apples comparison, but the game called "Influence" relied heavily on this similar concept. The hope was that with enough resources and enough recipes, nobody would reasonably be able to tackle the entire loop themselves. Unfortunately, that was quickly proven to be a non-issue for players and most serious players could be fully self sufficient - it was just a matter of working out that initial production flow.

author: [SLY] Groove [STAR]
content: I suppose, in my eyes, this isn't a binary game design decision. By that I mean that we're unlikely to make it impossible for someone to be fully horizontally and vertically integrated. But we can make it sufficiently difficult that on average, most players will be motivated to engage in exchange with other players for operations. However, scale of VWAP is going to play a considerable role here, which is also safeguarded through the Council Rank system.

author: Kr1gs
content: Laws and Rules: These would consist of gameplay mechanics that let players define political logic within areas for which they have PVP stake. It could be a wide range of items. For example, perhaps a locality designates their region to be a "no-combat" zone, even in MRZ. Now, there won't be any implicit restriction on combat, but that could be the voted upon law of the land.   Which takes us to point two. If you want to enforce a no combat zone, you'll need to either patrol that area and take action against players breaking the rules (laws), or hire NPC security forces to patrol that region for you. I anticipate every localized DAO to have some form of treasury funded by collecting some form of taxation for the area. Diplomats (PVP holders) assigned to those areas can decide how to spend from that treasury. They might decide to distribute funds out to themselves, or they might decide to "hire" NPC fleets to patrol

author: Icy
content: I like it because it would force what would otherwise be extractors from the game (by simply holding PVP) to purchase game assets -- at a minimum, crew. But the crew would also need to be transported to the region people want to deploy their stake. So, they can either purchase ships to transport crew, or hire someone else to do so.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: So yes, there's a benefit to ATLAS price going up. But using revenue to buy tokens is a terrible idea. We have a massive production budget. Using that capital to build future features is far more beneficial. More features also equals more players, in theory.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: Our formual is: Release great products -> grow the playerbase -> incentivize locking and reinvestment into game assets. Those are all drivers for token appreciation while not wasting money on pumpanomics

author: Kr1gs
content: Generally, I do think that relationship holds. Particularly in a growing or stable user environment. Part of the problem we're facing now is that there was massive overhead of token speculators (traders) that were holding the tokens and have sold off over time (not to account for the FTX liquidator), and attrition in the existing userbase with those people selling off tokens. Coupled with immature tokenomic systems that we intend to improve in future releases that further incentivize locking.

reply.preview: Generally, I do think that relationship holds. Particularly in a growing or stable user environment. Part of the problem we're facing now is that there was massive overhead of token speculators (traders) that were holding the tokens and have sold off over time (not to account for the FTX liquidator), and attrition in the existing userbase with those people selling off tokens. Coupled with immature tokenomic systems that we intend to improve in future releases that further incentivize locking.

author: [COEX] NDR
content: Sure, but these are still very immature tokenomic systems. We're constantly compromising on whether we build game features or tokenomics features. We typically alternate our development priority between the two. Which is why I was highlighting some of the next efforts being ATLAS Locker Addendum and POLIS integration into the game.  Super simple math as an example. If we grow to 50k DAUs, which is very achievable imo, and they all aspire to 72M ATLAS locked because of the benefits we layer in, that takes 3.6B ATLAS off market. 10% of the supply.  edit: Good catch by @[COEX] NDR -- 500 players maxing out the locker would absorb 100% of the total supply

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content:
Fair enough. But I was referring more to the hyperinflationary ponzinomic F2P web3 games. Has been proven as unsustainable. In any system with sufficient value distributed to pure extractors, it will be maximally extracted and destroy any value. 

Yes, we did add some light rewards to UE gameplay that can be earned without owning any assets. However, that value distribution is quite low relative to the time investment necessary. I'm not really tracking Ink prices, but I think it's either stable or going up? But, if we're being honest with ourselves, those Ink rewards aren't presently serving us for new user acquisition. Don't necessarily quote me on this, because our data analytics on UE are a bit broken right now. But I'm fairly confident we aren't seeing a lot of new users. Hypothetically, we could significantly boost the Ink rewards for gameplay, which would then likely bring more new people in to extract -- but the net result would almost certainly be a destruction of the value of Ink.

reply.preview: Message could not be loaded

author: ZiggyMUD
𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: @SW4GN3R if you believe we’re in the midst or close to a technological singularity, with AI capabilities advancing exponentially, and you’re confident in the product you’re building, isn’t there a risk of developing too slowly? Couldn’t competitors quickly replicate any advantage you create, especially with future AI advancements? In an open-source environment like crypto, you’ve mentioned that intellectual property (IP) is where the value lies. However, isn’t there a risk that other IPs, powered by AI, could surpass yours without a well-established user base of, say, 10,000 players? I’m trying to understand your perspective on balancing development speed with the rapid pace of AI advancements and where raising funds to speed up development come together?  I understand product development, quality, and functionality likely contribute to capital raise valuations, and timing is critical.  I'm just listening to many expert podcasts and the speed of AI development is mind-blowing. I very much understand that ATMTA is leveraging AI to the highest degree, but just curious on your general thoughts.

author: ZiggyMUD
content: It's a valid question.  The flipside of that is that ATMTA can (and should) start building 10X AI tools that can 10x development speed while maintaining quality.  The SES system Danny built is a great example of that.  I've built and continue to build 10x systems in my company.  There is definitely a real race going on.  IMO Companies that build the most powerful internal AI systems will win in many markets.

author: SW4GN3R [STAR]
content: "Entrepreneurs have to keep building, cycle in, cycle out. My advice to them: focus on product, retain your team, treat your community well, and don't mistake your token price as your report card. Remember what you're building and why you're building it, and trust that if you build something that your users love, the market will ultimately reward you.   Just might take longer than you once thought." Haseeb from Dragonfly

author: [SAI] Sim [STAR]
content:
I feel like that’s going to be pretty difficult to do. Fleets can just go back to their owner, and the worst thing that can happen is losing the cargo.
For claim stakes it’s different — you build on them, and I assume the upkeep you pay (in ATLAS) for the buildings scales with their level/tier.
Just like people in the real world don’t invest in a rented home since they can move out any day (They don’t renovate the piping nor install solar panels, for example), I don’t think it makes much sense to invest in a claim stake if you’re only keeping it for 24 days.
In that case, though, the renter should be able to sign a contract agreeing in advance on how many days they want to rent the CS for, instead of having a variable duration.
Just throwing ideas, of course.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content:
Well, I don't find the game all that fun. But I have to ask, why do we need to figure out how to fill lobbies? Maybe they need to have dynamically created matches. You sit in queue while you wait for enough people waiting and load in. While you wait it's a sort of match itself. 

Actually that works well with what I was thinking earlier. If you dynamically filled matches then you'd not have empty lobbies. Now imagine if there's two types of matches (lobby) and ranked. Have EXP that you collected prior to INK "conversion" be more like a droppable currency. When you get killed you drop some. Whoever picks it up gets it. Add a game winning streak counter (think Trials of Osiris in Destiny). The higher you get for wins the bigger your multiplier. At the end of the timeframe you take your exp and your multiplier and convert that to INK. As a player, when you first load in to that lobby match you can decided to queue for the "trials". While in lobby you don't get any EXP for kills. But maybe for skill? Hit accuracy, k/d for entire session, etc. Just some thoughts. If a lobby fills up with people that aren't queuing for "trials" spin up another lobby for more players.

author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: Because with no SBMM (which is useless now, due to low population) depending on what type of player you are, you have 2 choice:  -Hardcore gamer:-  1) You play however you want, don’t care about others, and just crush everyone to get as much INK as possible in the shortest time. 2) You try not to crush everyone, slow down to let others have fun, and end up leaving a good chunk of INK on the table (and you get bored eventually and you quit).  -Casual player:- 1) You get crushed by so many players that you quit after a few games because you’re not having fun. 2) You keep playing just for entertainment, but you earn little to no INK.  In every scenarios, I dont see how you can create a sticky gameplay right now.

reply.preview: Because with no SBMM (which is useless now, due to low population) depending on what type of player you are, you have 2 choice: -Hardcore gamer:- 1) You play however you want, don’t care about others, and just crush everyone to get as much INK as possible in the shortest time. 2) You try not to crush everyone, slow down to let others have fun, and end up leaving a good chunk of INK on the table (and you get bored eventually and you quit). -Casual player:- 1) You get crushed by so many players that you quit after a few games because you’re not having fun. 2) You keep playing just for entertainment, but you earn little to no INK. In every scenarios, I dont see how you can create a sticky gameplay right now.

author: 《VΛ》 KATIREcon Vantarah
content: i kinda want to test my ai on that, but better some team member confirms: Based on the information available regarding the C4 update mechanics, the Faction Loyalty Point (LP) pool is directly affected by the number of regions a faction controls, though the number of Tier 5 Starbases (SBs) plays a critical role in establishing that control at the time of the C4 launch. Here is a detailed breakdown of how faction control influences the LP pool: LP Pool Determination (Regions Controlled) The core mechanic dictating the Atlas emissions available to a faction's LP pool is linked to the state of the map and the number of regions a faction owns. • Function of Regions: Each faction will have an Atlas emissions pool that is a function of the number of regions they control on the map. • Diminishing Returns: The design incorporates a system of diminishing marginal returns to region control. This means the first region a faction conquers is the most valuable region they will ever receive in terms of their Atlas LP emissions. • Balancing Mechanism: This structure creates a natural balancing mechanism. For example, if one faction completely dominates the map, controlling 50 regions, the total Atlas emissions pool (the red line in the simulation) actually goes down compared to the optimal distribution where all factions control an equal number of regions (23 regions each). The total Atlas emissions peak at around 8.15 million Atlas when control is equally split among the 69 regions (23 regions per faction). Role of Tier 5 Starbases in C4 Launch While the LP emission rate is tied to region control, the starbase tiers achieved in the current Starbased game determine a faction's initial territory control upon the C4 mainnet launch. • Region Control Requirement: To gain ownership of a region in C4, a faction must control all of the Core systems within that region. • Tier 5 Advantage: For any starbase that a faction has upgraded to Tier 5 in the current Starbased system, all of the core systems within that region will be captured on that faction's behalf at the C4 launch. This means that the region is secured for the faction from the start without requiring immediate military action to capture the core systems individually. • Lower Tier Status: If a starbase is Tier 4 or lower at the time of the C4 mainnet launch, players will have to enter that region and capture all the core systems before the faction owns the region. In summary, the number of regions controlled is the calculation input for the LP pool. The effort invested in upgrading Starbases to Tier 5 directly translates into starting control over regions in C4, which in turn secures the baseline for the faction's LP emissions pool.

author: 𝐋ᴀɴᴢᴇʀ [SPS]
content:
Not sure who this goes to. UE5. I figured out that the constant crashing of my game is due to my audio setup with BEACN Mix Create. The error code is mostlyMMDevAPI  I uninstalled BEACN Mix and it stopped the crashes. Request a fix on that. Need BEACN mix for streaming and audio management.

Unhandled Exception: EXCEPTION_ACCESS_VIOLATION 0x0000000000000000
MMDevAPI
kernel32
ntdll

Chat GPT response: The error EXCEPTION_ACCESS_VIOLATION reading address 0x0000000000000038  is very similar to the last one. It means the game tried to read a tiny bit of data (specifically, 56 bytes past address zero) from a memory location it wasn't allowed to, likely because it was expecting valid information about an audio device but got bad data instead. This usually happens when the state of an audio device changes unexpectedly or if there's a driver conflict.

Looking at your StarAtlas.log, I see the game initializes several audio systems (Wwise , XAudio2 ) and specifically identifies "Game (3- BEACN Mix Create)" as your output device. The BEACN Mix Create is a fairly complex audio interface with its own software and drivers. It's possible there's a conflict between it, the game (Star Atlas ), Unreal Engine, and the Windows MMDevAPI

author: ZiggyMUD
content: Good, but we probably won't notice. Protocols have mostly adapted.  Good.  Basically anyone trying to get historical data was a massive PITA.   So a lot of protocols would start "now" and then store future (now) data locally in databases.  Which is why sometimes you would see only now/forward looking protocols.  Bad - It's gated behind Helius who likely built a local database as described above.  And it's not an on chain improvement so only Helius can access it.  If Helius g oes down your protocol is boned.

author: Odicerous
content:
That is so awesome!  Many, memories.
I started my kid on the original, New Hope.  He was so scared of Vader, he hid his face behind pillows.  Wouldn't finish the movie.  Took a couple of days to get through it.  I was sad.
 Six months later, he'd watched every movie, twice.  Then all the Clone Wars, then all the spin offs, then everything professionally produced, and every amateur hack on Youtube.
We had 5 long years of daily diatribes about what was canon and what wasn't, what was  REALLY happening in the empire, and what Disney was  getting right and what they were missing.We had Empire Recruitment Posters posted around the house, lego star wars characters on every mantle, and Bad Batch recaps with breakfast. 
Be very careful opening Pandora's box.

content:
@[HEIM] Loki 
⁠😎┃foundation-room⁠
You may not have the same legal issues in your country.
But in the USA, people and companies get sued and lose money all the time.
We live in (and ATMTA operates from) a very litigious society.
Therefore, we have become accustomed to waiving many of our rights when using any platform.
We can't have SA right now without this.
But perhaps it won't last forever and one day SA will be a true cybernation of its own.
Personally, I don't think you need to worry about the Terms of Service.
If you have trusted ATMTA to take care of our community thus far, you can probably trust them in Z.ink.
I very much doubt they will ever freeze assets unless it is crystal clear that those assets were stolen.
Just my humble opinion.

author: odvb
content:
The team announced the surprise launch of the Z.ink Alpha Season, which is a 6-week test period for earning ZXP ( Z.ink  XP) that will lead into the token airdrop campaign.
Key Points on Earning ZXP and Benefits
• Earning ZXP: ZXP is earned through various activities within the Star Atlas ecosystem, including playing Sage, playing HoloSim, making purchases on the primary marketplace, completing Unreal Engine (UE5) matches, and locking Atlas or Polis tokens.
    ◦ Note: XP from locking tokens updates once per day (similar to Sage epochs), not in real time.
    ◦ Prior XP does not count; all ZXP accrual starts now, ensuring a "fair launch".
• XP Multipliers and Boosts:
    ◦ Maintaining a daily check-in streak for 15 days grants a 25% XP boost. This streak carries forward into the next season.
    ◦ Purchasing the  Z.ink  Club premium subscription (Multi Pass) provides additional multipliers, potentially up to 2x more ZXP, and other perks.
• Alpha Benefits (Carry-Over): Although this is a test season, participants gain a significant head start for the subsequent mainnet season:
    ◦ A portion of earned XP (up to Level 20, adjusted for any leveling curve changes) will carry forward.
    ◦ The achieved daily streak/XP boost carries forward.
    ◦ Participants receive cosmetic rewards.
Roadmap and Network Future
• Origins Season (Mainnet Campaign): Following the 6-week alpha season and a short downtime (1-2 weeks), the Origins Season (expected to run about 3 months) will begin.
    ◦ 10% of the total  Z.ink  token supply is allocated to the Origins Season, distributed proportionally based on total ZXP earned.
• Mainnet Launch: The ultimate goal is for the Zinc mainnet and Sage C4 (a major Star Atlas game update) to launch simultaneously at the conclusion of the Origins Season.
• Permissionless Network: The  Z.ink  blockchain will be fully open access and permissionless, allowing any team to build applications and allowing users to run validator nodes.
• Staking Incentives: Staking  Z.ink tokens will be highly incentivized early on, with an initial inflation rate starting at 12% that decays annually over an eight-year period.
• Unique Value Proposition:  Z.ink is being built as a digital identity and reputation system with strong "sybil resistance" (confirming real individual users). This allows the network to partner with other Web3 projects (including games or DeFi platforms) to conduct highly targeted airdrops based on a user’s reputation, level, and activity.

This streak carries forward into the next season. ◦ Purchasing the Z.ink Club premium subscription (Multi Pass) provides additional multipliers, potentially up to 2x more ZXP, and other perks. • Alpha Benefits (Carry-Over): Although this is a test season, participants gain a significant head start for the subsequent mainnet season: ◦ A portion of earned XP (up to Level 20, adjusted for any leveling curve changes) will carry forward. ◦ The achieved daily streak/XP boost carries forward. ◦ Participants receive cosmetic rewards. Roadmap and Network Future • Origins Season (Mainnet Campaign): Following the 6-week alpha season and a short downtime (1-2 weeks), the Origins Season (expected to run about 3 months) will begin. ◦ 10% of the total Z.ink token supply is allocated to the Origins Season, distributed proportionally based on total ZXP earned. • Mainnet Launch: The ultimate goal is for the Zinc mainnet and Sage C4 (a major Star Atlas game update) to launch simultaneously at the conclusion of the Origins Season. • Permissionless Network: The Z.ink blockchain will be fully open access and permissionless, allowing any team to build applications and allowing users to run validator nodes. • Staking Incentives: Staking Z.ink tokens will be highly incentivized early on, with an initial inflation rate starting at 12% that decays annually over an eight-year period. • Unique Value Proposition: Z.ink is being built as a digital identity and reputation system with strong "sybil resistance" (confirming real individual users). This allows the network to partner with other Web3 projects (including games or DeFi platforms) to conduct highly targeted airdrops based on a user’s reputation, level, and activity.

author: [Λ] Neo_AArmstrong [EC]Econ Vantarah
content:
I don't have in-depth technical knowledge to make a definitive statement, but I'll speak in layman's terms, which is how best I can express myself.
The SA MP sells generic items, meaning they are all the same token, whether it's the ships (all identical airbikes) or the crews (an unrevealed Ana Tolle or a crew pack).
When you reveal the pack, you determine the NFT attributes, which prevents the system from listing in batches as it does today, requiring a more robust system for individual listings, like Tensor or Magic Eden.
